mergeRationale: The change removes the server-side RS256 allowlist and lets an undefined key reach jwt.verify, moving a required startup configuration failure into the request path and weakening authentication's fail-closed boundary.

requiredFindings.summary: The patch removes the RS256 algorithms allowlist and lets the token choose the verification algorithm.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: Authentication must fail closed and the server must decide which algorithms are acceptable. Removing algorithms: ['RS256'] changes the verifier from a pinned asymmetric-token contract to whatever jwt.verify derives from the token/header and supplied key, so issuer/audience checks no longer prove the signature was verified under the intended algorithm.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Keep algorithms pinned to RS256 and use the configured public key for production tokens; if local development needs a different key, make that an explicit, separately pinned configuration.

requiredFindings.summary: process.env.JWT_SECRET || undefined turns a missing required key into a request-time verifier input instead of a startup failure.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: Required authentication configuration should be validated when the process starts. Passing undefined into jwt.verify delays a must-fail configuration error until token verification and creates a fail-open risk at the auth boundary.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Validate required keys at startup and refuse to start or reject all auth requests when the key configuration is missing.

requiredFindings.summary: The added test only covers a dev happy path, not malicious or misconfigured tokens.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: Auth changes need negative tests: wrong algorithm, missing key, wrong issuer and wrong audience, plus a startup-configuration test.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Add negative token tests and a startup configuration (fail-closed) test.

disallowedConclusions: The patch can be merged because the local dev test passes and issuer/audience are still checked.
Reading the key from JWT_SECRET is fine because production still sets a strong secret.
